title: Range-controlled entanglement in Lindbladian skin states of monitored fermions
authors: Gianluca Passarelli, Angelo Russomanno, Davide Rossini, Procolo Lucignano
abstract: Reservoir engineering can stabilize states inaccessible to unitary dynamics. Directed particle-conserving dissipation creates Lindbladian skin states, where Pauli exclusion turns edge accumulation into a many-body density imbalance. In a monitored fermion chain with tunable hopping range, we identify, within a Gaussian trajectory approximation, two finite-size scaling regimes: short-range hopping is consistent with complete skin accumulation and area-law entanglement, whereas sufficiently long-range hopping produces a finite bulk tail and effective algebraic sub-volume-law entanglement. Dissipation and coherent hopping thus jointly control skin localization and quantum entanglement, highlighting their close interconnection.
summary: The research explores the interplay between directed dissipation and coherent hopping in fermionic chains. By tuning the range of particle hopping ($\alpha$), the system can be driven into distinct steady states characterized by either a complete skin effect or an incomplete density tail. This physical control over localization is shown to directly dictate the scaling law of quantum entanglement, linking non-Hermitian physics to quantum information theory.
main_problem: The study investigates how tuning the hopping range ($\alpha$) controls both the localization of particle density (Lindbladian Skin Effect) and the scaling behavior of quantum entanglement in monitored fermionic systems.
main_result: Short-range hopping leads to a complete skin effect accompanied by area-law entanglement, while long-range hopping results in an incomplete skin tail and algebraic sub-volume-law entanglement.
method: The dynamics are simulated using the master equation unraveled into quantum jump trajectories, analyzed within a Gaussian trajectory approximation for correlation matrices.
model_or_system: The system is modeled by spinless fermions on an open chain evolving under a Lindbladian dissipation ($\mathcal{L}$) and a coherent hopping Hamiltonian ($\hat{H}_\alpha$) with tunable range $\alpha$.
key_observables: Particle density imbalance ($\langle\hat{I}angle$), half-chain entanglement entropy ($S_L/2$), and the scaling exponent ($ eta$) of $S(L)$.
important_parameters: Hopping range parameter $\alpha$ (controlling hopping decay), dissipation asymmetry $\gamma$, and coherent coupling strength $\lambda$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on a Gaussian trajectory approximation to manage the complexity of higher-order correlation functions during quantum jump simulations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate steady-state density profiles showing sharp fronts (short-range) versus extended tails (long-range), and plot entanglement entropy scaling behavior for different hopping ranges and boundary conditions.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares the outcomes of short-range ($\alpha 	o \infty$) vs. long-range ($1 < \alpha < 2$) hopping regimes, demonstrating how $\alpha$ dictates whether a complete skin effect (area law) or an incomplete tail (sub-volume law) emerges.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete example of how non-Hermitian physics (the skin effect) couples directly to quantum information measures like entanglement entropy, offering tunable control over many-body correlations via microscopic parameters.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Representative steady-state fermion density profiles at half filling for γ = 1/2 and λ = 2. The shaded region marks the left half of the chain, where directed dissipation accumulates particles. (a) Short-range hopping sharpens the Fermi-skin profile with increasing L (see curve labels), consis- tent with complete skin accumulation; the inset sketches the corresponding area-law behavior of the trajectory entangle- ment entropy. (b) Long-range hopping leaves a finite density tail in the right half of the chain over the accessible sizes, in- dicating incomplete skin accumulation; the inset sketches the corresponding sub-volume-law finite-size growth of entangle- ment.

FIG. 3. Finite-size difference δSL/2 for (a) the nearest- neighbor model (α →∞), (b) α = 3/2, (c) α = 1, and (d) α = 1/2. The onset of entanglement growth drifts toward larger λ with increasing L for α →∞and, over the accessible sizes, also for α = 3/2, while it remains approximately size- independent for α = 1 and α = 1/2.

FIG. 2. Data for the nearest-neighbor Hamiltonian (γ = 1/2): (a) imbalance I and (c) half-chain entanglement entropy SL/2 as functions of the coherent coupling λ. (b) Imbalance and (d) half-chain entanglement entropy as functions of the system size L. In (d), solid lines show fits to Eq. (6). Within the reliable fitting window, they yield β ≥1, consistent with the absence of algebraic growth.

FIG. 4. (a,b) Data for the long-range Hamiltonian with α = 1/2 (γ = 1/2): (a) imbalance I and (b) half-chain entanglement entropy SL/2 as functions of the coherent coupling λ. (c) Fitting exponent β [Eq. (6)] as a function of the hopping range α for representative values of λ. The dashed line marks β = 1. The minimum near α ≃1 indicates maximal sub-volume-law growth, while the recovery of β ≥1 occurs within the interval 1 ≲α ≲2.
